title: Therefore, Māluṅkyaputta, remember what I have left unanswered as unanswered, and what I have answered as answered. And what, Māluṅkyaputta, have I left unanswered? 'The…
content: Therefore, Māluṅkyaputta, remember what I have left unanswered as unanswered, and what I have answered as answered. And what, Māluṅkyaputta, have I left unanswered? 'The world is eternal' — I have left unanswered. 'The world is not eternal' — I have left unanswered. 'The world is finite' — I have left unanswered. 'The world is infinite' — I have left unanswered. 'The soul and the body are the same' — I have left unanswered. 'The soul and the body are different' — I have left unanswered. 'The Tathāgata exists after death' — I have left unanswered. 'The Tathāgata does not exist after death' — I have left unanswered. And why have I left these unanswered? Because they are not connected with the goal, they are not fundamental to the holy life, they do not lead to disenchantment, dispassion, cessation, peace, direct knowledge, enlightenment, Nibbāna. Therefore I have left them unanswered. And what, Māluṅkyaputta, have I answered? 'This is suffering' — I have answered. 'This is the origin of suffering' — I have answered. 'This is the cessation of suffering' — I have answered. 'This is the path leading to the cessation of suffering' — I have answered. And why have I answered these? Because they are connected with the goal, they are fundamental to the holy life, they lead to disenchantment, dispassion, cessation, peace, direct knowledge, enlightenment, Nibbāna. Therefore I have answered them.

title: Venerable sirs, if a bhikkhu, examining thus, knows: 'I dwell much with a greedy mind; I dwell much with a hateful mind; I dwell much…
content: Venerable sirs, if a bhikkhu, examining thus, knows: 'I dwell much with a greedy mind; I dwell much with a hateful mind; I dwell much overcome by dullness and drowsiness; I dwell much with restlessness; I dwell much with doubt; I dwell much with anger; I dwell much with a defiled mind; I dwell much with bodily fever; I dwell much with laziness; I dwell much with an unconcentrated mind,' then that bhikkhu should arouse determination, desire, energy, effort, exertion, non-backsliding, mindfulness, and clear comprehension to abandon those evil unwholesome states. Just as, venerable sirs, when a cloth is on fire or the head is on fire, one should arouse determination, desire, energy, effort, exertion, non-backsliding, mindfulness, and clear comprehension to extinguish the burning cloth or head. So too, venerable sirs, that bhikkhu should arouse determination, desire, energy, effort, exertion, non-backsliding, mindfulness, and clear comprehension to abandon those evil unwholesome states. Venerable sirs, if a bhikkhu, examining thus, knows: 'I dwell much with a mind without greed; I dwell much with a mind without hatred; I dwell much with a mind without dullness and drowsiness; I dwell much with a mind without restlessness; I dwell much with a mind beyond doubt; I dwell much with a mind without anger; I dwell much with an undefiled mind; I dwell much with a body without fever; I dwell much with an energetic and striving mind; I dwell much with a concentrated mind,' then that bhikkhu, having established himself in those wholesome states, should further focus on the destruction of the taints.

title: Possessing three qualities, bhikkhus, a fool is known. What three? He asks questions unwisely; he answers questions unwisely; when another answers a question wisely with…
content: Possessing three qualities, bhikkhus, a fool is known. What three? He asks questions unwisely; he answers questions unwisely; when another answers a question wisely with well-rounded words and phrases, with smooth, polished prose, he does not accept it. Possessing these three qualities, bhikkhus, a fool is known. Possessing three qualities, bhikkhus, a wise person is known. What three? He asks questions wisely; he answers questions wisely; when another answers a question wisely with well-rounded words and phrases, with smooth, polished prose, he accepts it. Possessing these three qualities, bhikkhus, a wise person is known.

title: Bhikkhus, a bhikkhu possessing two things dwells happily in this very life, without trouble, without distress, without fever; and after death, with the breakup of…
content:
Bhikkhus, a bhikkhu possessing two things dwells happily in this very life, without trouble, without distress, without fever; and after death, with the breakup of the body, a good destination is to be expected. What two? Restraint of the sense faculties and moderation in eating. Bhikkhus, a bhikkhu possessing these two things dwells happily in this very life... a good destination is to be expected.

Eye, ear, and nose,
Tongue, body, and mind —
A bhikkhu who guards these doors well,
Who has moderation in eating,
Experiences bodily happiness,
Experiences mental happiness.
Burning in body,
Burning in mind,
Whether by day or by night,
He dwells in happiness.

title: Friends, if a bhikkhu requests: 'May the venerable ones speak to me, may I be spoken to by the venerable ones!' But if he is…
content: Friends, if a bhikkhu requests: 'May the venerable ones speak to me, may I be spoken to by the venerable ones!' But if he is difficult to speak to, possessing qualities that make him difficult to speak to, hard to endure, not respectfully accepting instruction — then his fellow celibates think that bhikkhu is not worth speaking to, think that bhikkhu is not worth instructing, think that one should not place confidence in him.

title: Dhamma-conduct and the holy life are called the best conduct. If one goes forth from home to homelessness, Yet if he is harsh of speech,…
content:
Dhamma-conduct and the holy life are called the best conduct.
If one goes forth from home to homelessness,
Yet if he is harsh of speech, fond of harming like a beast,
His life is evil, he increases the dust of defilements.

A bhikkhu who loves disputes, covered by ignorance,
Does not understand the Dhamma and Discipline taught by the Buddha.

Led by ignorance, he harms those trained in self-development,
Not knowing the defiled path that leads to hell.

Such a bhikkhu, fallen into a bad destination,
From this birth to that, from darkness to darkness,
After death comes to suffering.

title: Bhikkhu, practice meditation, Do not be heedless, Do not let your mind whirl in sensual pleasures. Do not, being heedless, swallow a red-hot iron ball,…
content:
Bhikkhu, practice meditation,
Do not be heedless,
Do not let your mind whirl in sensual pleasures.
Do not, being heedless, swallow a red-hot iron ball,
And when burning, cry 'This is suffering!'

There is no jhāna without wisdom,
There is no wisdom without jhāna.
One who has both jhāna and wisdom
Is indeed close to Nibbāna.

Entering an empty hut,
The bhikkhu with peaceful mind
Enjoys a superhuman delight
When he sees the Dhamma clearly.

Whenever one is mindful
Of the rise and fall of the aggregates,
One experiences joy and happiness.
That is the Deathless for those who know.

title: Bhikkhus, as long as the bhikkhus share without partiality, together with their virtuous companions, all righteous, lawful gains, even down to the contents of their…
content: Bhikkhus, as long as the bhikkhus share without partiality, together with their virtuous companions, all righteous, lawful gains, even down to the contents of their alms bowls, then the bhikkhus will prosper and not decline.

title: Rāhula, for one who knowingly speaks falsely, without shame and fear, I say there is no evil he will not do. Therefore, Rāhula, 'I will…
content: Rāhula, for one who knowingly speaks falsely, without shame and fear, I say there is no evil he will not do. Therefore, Rāhula, 'I will not speak falsely, even for a joke' — thus, Rāhula, you should train.

title: Furthermore, bhikkhus, a bhikkhu, when walking for alms in the village or town, does not receive coarse or fine food as much as he wishes.…
content: Furthermore, bhikkhus, a bhikkhu, when walking for alms in the village or town, does not receive coarse or fine food as much as he wishes. He thinks: 'I walked for alms in the village or town and did not receive coarse or fine food as much as I wished. Now my body is tired and cannot bear it. Let me lie down.' He lies down, not making an effort... unrealized. Bhikkhus, this is the fifth ground for laziness.
